title: Customer Support Triage Automation
problem: Support team manually classified 1,800 inbound tickets per week across 14 categories. Average triage time was 8 minutes per ticket; wrong routing caused 22% rework.
conditions_for_success: Minimum 6 months of labelled historical tickets
Clear category taxonomy agreed before build
Dedicated Zendesk admin for integration
anti_patterns: Do not use when ticket volume is under 200 per week
Avoid if taxonomy changes more than quarterly
generalized_for: Any company with high-volume inbound requests that need routing across multiple specialist teams, regardless of industry.
lessons_learned: Initial classifier hit 91% accuracy on top-5 categories but only 64% on the long-tail 9 categories — had to add a confidence threshold and route low-confidence tickets to a human triage queue, which added 2 weeks to the timeline.
22% rework rate was actually caused by only 3 of the 14 categories being ambiguously defined — fixing taxonomy definitions alone dropped rework to 14% before the model even launched.
Agents initially distrusted the automation and re-triaged 40% of auto-routed tickets for the first 3 weeks; required a parallel-run period showing 94% agreement before adoption stabilized.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that triage speed was the bottleneck. Discovery revealed the real cost was rework from misrouting — agents spent 18 minutes on rerouted tickets vs 8 minutes on correctly routed ones. The ROI case shifted from 'faster triage' to 'eliminate rework loops'.
implementation_friction: Zendesk webhook configuration required IT security review — 3 week delay for firewall rule changes.
Historical ticket labels were inconsistent across 3 team leads who used different naming conventions — 2 weeks spent on label harmonization before training data was usable.
Salesforce integration required a custom middleware because the Zendesk-Salesforce native connector didn't support the custom fields needed — added 1.5 weeks.

title: Real-Time Fraud Detection Copilot
problem: Fraud analysts reviewed 600 flagged transactions per day manually. False positive rate was 68%, burning analyst time and delaying legitimate transactions by 4 hours.
conditions_for_success: Historical transaction data with fraud labels available (2+ years)
Real-time data pipeline already in place
Compliance sign-off on AI-assisted decisioning
anti_patterns: Do not fully automate decisions without human in loop for high-value transactions
Avoid if regulatory environment prohibits unexplainable model outputs
generalized_for: Any company with a high-volume manual review queue where most flagged items are false positives, such as content moderation, compliance screening, or insurance claims.
lessons_learned: The existing rule-based system generated 68% false positives, but 12% of those false positives were actually edge-case fraud that the rules caught by accident — the copilot initially missed these, requiring a hybrid approach that kept 4 legacy rules alongside the model.

Analysts trusted the copilot more for low-value transactions (<$500) within 2 weeks, but refused to rely on it for transactions over $10K for 6 weeks until they saw enough correct predictions — adoption was value-tier dependent.
Real-time latency requirement of <200ms forced a model simplification that dropped accuracy by 3 points compared to the batch prototype — the team had to accept the tradeoff.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that the model needed to catch more fraud. Discovery showed the bigger ROI was reducing false positives — each false positive delayed a legitimate transaction by 4 hours and cost $23 in analyst time, totaling $4.1M/year in wasted effort. Fraud losses were $1.2M/year. The false positive problem was 3.4x more expensive than the fraud problem.
implementation_friction: Compliance team required a 6-week parallel run with full audit logging before any production traffic could flow through the copilot — extended timeline by 4 weeks beyond initial plan.
Snowflake query latency for feature computation was 800ms, too slow for real-time scoring — required building a Redis feature cache, adding 2 weeks of engineering.

Fraud label quality was poor: 8% of 'confirmed fraud' labels in historical data were actually chargebacks from buyer's remorse, not true fraud — spent 1.5 weeks cleaning labels with the fraud ops team.

title: Compliance Document Review Acceleration
problem: Compliance team spent 3 weeks per quarter manually reviewing 400+ policy documents for regulatory changes. Two FTEs dedicated entirely to this task.
conditions_for_success: Documents stored in searchable digital format
Compliance team available to validate model outputs weekly
anti_patterns: Do not use as sole review mechanism without human sign-off
Avoid if document formats are highly inconsistent (scans, handwriting)
generalized_for: Any regulated organization that must periodically review a large corpus of internal documents against changing external requirements — applies to legal, pharma, insurance, and government contractors.
lessons_learned: 58% of the 400 policy documents had no material changes quarter-over-quarter — the biggest time savings came from confidently identifying 'no change needed' documents, not from analyzing the changed ones.

The system initially flagged 35 false regulatory changes per cycle because it confused internal policy updates with external regulatory changes — required adding a source classification layer that added 1 week but cut false flags to 4.

Two of the 4 missed-change incidents in the prior year were caused by documents stored in a separate SharePoint site that nobody told the compliance team about — the system's document crawl actually discovered these orphaned documents.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that AI should help analyze regulatory changes faster. Discovery revealed the real bottleneck was finding which of 400+ documents were affected by a given change — the search problem was 3x harder than the analysis problem. Switched focus from 'smarter analysis' to 'smarter retrieval and matching'.
implementation_friction: SharePoint permissions model required per-document access grants for the service account — IT took 2.5 weeks to provision access across 6 document libraries.

Confluence and SharePoint had different versioning semantics — a 'current version' in Confluence could be a draft, while in SharePoint it was always published — required normalization logic that took 1 week.
Compliance team was only available for validation sessions on Fridays, which serialized the feedback loop — every iteration took a minimum of 1 week regardless of fix complexity.

title: Patient Scheduling Optimization
problem: Outpatient clinic had 31% no-show rate and average scheduling coordinator handling 90 calls per day. 18% of slots were double-booked or left unfilled.
conditions_for_success: EHR system has API access (Epic FHIR preferred)
Patient contact data is clean and opt-in for SMS
Scheduling rules documented and stable
anti_patterns: Do not automate complex multi-specialist referral scheduling without care coordinator review
Avoid if patient population has low smartphone/SMS access
generalized_for: Any appointment-based business with high no-show rates and manual scheduling overhead — applies to dental practices, auto repair shops, legal consultations, and salon chains.
lessons_learned: The no-show prediction model achieved 78% accuracy, but the 22% false predictions caused patient frustration when overbooking occurred — had to cap overbooking at 10% of slots and use waitlist backfill instead, which reduced the no-show impact from 42% to a still-strong 38%.

SMS reminder timing mattered enormously: 48-hour reminders reduced no-shows by 28%, but adding a same-day-morning reminder pushed it to 42% — the second reminder was responsible for nearly half the improvement and cost almost nothing extra.

18% double-booking rate was caused by 3 coordinators using a separate paper calendar for walk-in slots that wasn't synced to Epic — fixing the process eliminated 80% of double-bookings before any AI was deployed.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that smarter scheduling algorithms would fill more slots. Discovery showed the primary issue was no-shows creating cascading gaps — a slot vacated by a no-show at 9am couldn't be refilled by 9am. Shifted focus from 'optimal initial scheduling' to 'predictive overbooking plus real-time backfill from waitlist'.
implementation_friction: Epic FHIR API access required a 4-week vendor approval process and $12K in API licensing fees that weren't in the original budget.
Patient SMS opt-in rates were only 61% initially — spent 2 weeks running a front-desk campaign to boost opt-in to 84% before the system had enough coverage to be effective.
Twilio SMS deliverability issues with certain carriers caused 8% of reminders to fail silently — required adding delivery confirmation webhooks and a fallback to voice calls, adding 1.5 weeks.

title: Insurance Claims Processing Automation
problem: Claims adjudicators manually processed 2,400 claims per week with 14% error rate and 11-day average cycle time. Rework cost was $380K per year.
conditions_for_success: Claims data in standardized format (HL7 or CMS-1500)
Clear escalation path for edge cases to human adjudicators
IT security approval for PHI handling in ML pipeline
anti_patterns: Do not automate high-complexity claims without clinical review
Avoid if denial rates are already under audit
generalized_for: Any organization processing high-volume structured documents with decision rules — applies to loan underwriting, permit applications, warranty claims, and tax return processing.
lessons_learned: 71% straight-through processing rate was only achievable for claims under $5,000 — claims over $5,000 had a 34% edge-case rate that required human review, making the overall straight-through rate volume-weighted rather than uniform.

14% error rate was concentrated in 3 claim types (out of 22): mental health, durable medical equipment, and out-of-network — these 3 types accounted for 61% of all errors due to ambiguous coverage rules.

Rework cost of $380K/year included $140K in provider call-backs alone — automating the initial adjudication reduced provider inquiries by 52% because decisions came faster and with clearer explanations.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that AI should replace adjudicators for speed. Discovery showed the 11-day cycle time was 70% wait time (claims sitting in queue) and only 30% processing time — the real fix was auto-processing simple claims immediately to clear the queue, so human adjudicators could focus on complex claims without a backlog.
implementation_friction: PHI handling required a dedicated HIPAA-compliant ML pipeline environment — provisioning the secure GCP project took 3 weeks and involved legal, compliance, and IT security teams.

Salesforce Health Cloud's claims API had a 500-record batch limit that created processing bottlenecks — required building a queuing system to handle the 2,400 weekly claims in batches, adding 2 weeks.

Three different claim types used different PDF layouts from legacy systems — OCR extraction accuracy varied from 94% to 71% across formats, requiring format-specific extraction templates that added 1.5 weeks.

title: Clinical Documentation Assistant
problem: Physicians spent 2.4 hours per day on documentation after patient visits. Burnout scores were high; 3 physicians left in 18 months citing administrative burden.
conditions_for_success: Physicians have smartphones or tablets for voice input
EHR system supports ambient documentation integration
HIPAA BAA in place with all AI vendors
anti_patterns: Avoid ambient capture in sensitive mental health or substance abuse sessions without explicit consent protocols
Do not deploy without a physician champion leading adoption
generalized_for: Any professional services organization where highly-paid experts spend excessive time on post-engagement documentation — applies to law firms, consulting firms, audit teams, and field engineers.
lessons_learned: Ambient documentation captured 92% of relevant clinical details, but physicians spent 15 minutes per note editing the 8% that was wrong — the editing time was still less than the original 2.4 hours, but physician perception was that 'fixing AI mistakes' felt more frustrating than writing from scratch.

3 of 22 physicians refused to use the system for the first 4 weeks — the physician champion personally demonstrated their own time savings (1.8 hours/day), which converted all holdouts within 6 weeks.

Same-day note completion jumped from 34% to 75%, but the real financial impact was in coding accuracy — AI-drafted notes included more specific diagnostic codes, increasing average reimbursement per visit by $18.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that documentation time was the key problem. Discovery revealed the real cost was physician attrition — each departed physician cost $250K-$500K to replace (recruiting, onboarding, lost revenue). The 3 departures in 18 months cost more than all the documentation inefficiency combined. Reframed the project from 'save time' to 'prevent attrition'.
implementation_friction: HIPAA BAA negotiation with Nuance took 5 weeks due to custom data handling requirements — delayed project start by 3 weeks beyond planned.
Epic's ambient documentation API required version 2022.1+ but 4 clinic sites were on 2021.3 — coordinating the EHR upgrade across sites took 3 weeks and required weekend downtime windows.

Physician training sessions had to be scheduled during lunch hours (the only available time) — could only train 4-5 physicians per session, stretching the rollout over 4 weeks instead of the planned 1 week.

title: Dispatch Optimization for Regional Logistics
problem: Dispatch team manually assigned 380 daily routes across 90 drivers using spreadsheets. Average overtime per driver was 6 hours per week; fuel costs ran 12% over budget.
conditions_for_success: Fleet telematics data available in real-time (GPS + ETA)
Dispatcher buy-in — tool must be advisory, not forced
Delivery windows and constraints documented in structured format
anti_patterns: Do not fully automate routing for hazmat or high-value cargo without dispatcher approval
Avoid if delivery window data is unreliable or frequently changes same-day
generalized_for: Any business that assigns mobile workers to daily routes or job sites — applies to HVAC service companies, home healthcare providers, food delivery fleets, and field sales teams.
lessons_learned: The optimization model reduced total route miles by 14%, but dispatchers rejected 31% of suggested routes in the first 2 weeks because the model didn't account for driver-customer relationships — regular drivers knew client dock procedures and preferences. Added a 'driver-client affinity' constraint that reduced rejections to 8%.

Fuel savings of 9% were less than the projected 15% because the model optimized for distance, not fuel consumption — hilly routes with fewer miles sometimes used more fuel than flat routes with more miles. Adding elevation data improved fuel prediction accuracy but only recovered 2 more points.

Overtime reduction was primarily driven by better load balancing across drivers, not shorter routes — the top 15 drivers were getting 60% of the routes because dispatchers defaulted to 'who I trust', leaving other drivers underutilized.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that route optimization would save fuel. Discovery showed that 68% of the cost overrun was overtime, not fuel — dispatchers over-assigned experienced drivers while newer drivers sat idle. The real opportunity was load balancing across the driver pool, not finding shorter routes.
implementation_friction: Samsara API rate limits (60 requests/minute) were too low for real-time route recalculation across 90 drivers — required building a local cache that synced every 5 minutes, adding 1.5 weeks.
Delivery window data was stored in 3 different formats across 2 systems (SAP and a custom Access database) — normalizing delivery constraints took 2 weeks.

Two senior dispatchers with 15+ years experience actively resisted the tool for 3 weeks — required dedicated 1-on-1 sessions showing how the tool supported rather than replaced their expertise, costing 1 week of project time.

title: Invoice Exception Handling Automation
problem: AP team processed 9,000 invoices per month with 18% exception rate requiring manual research. Each exception averaged 22 minutes to resolve; 2 FTEs dedicated to exceptions only.
conditions_for_success: ERP system (SAP or Oracle) has integration capability
Supplier master data is clean and regularly maintained
Finance leadership committed to process standardization
anti_patterns: Do not automate exception resolution for contracts with complex penalty clauses
Avoid if supplier data quality is poor — fix data first
generalized_for: Any organization processing high-volume vendor invoices with a significant exception rate — applies to hospital systems, retail chains, construction firms, and government procurement offices.
lessons_learned: 61% of exceptions were caused by only 4 root causes: PO number mismatch (28%), quantity discrepancy (18%), price variance over tolerance (9%), and missing receiving confirmation (6%) — building specific handlers for these 4 cases covered the majority of exceptions without needing a general-purpose AI.

The $94K in early payment discount capture was an unexpected win — faster exception resolution meant invoices cleared the approval workflow 6 days sooner on average, catching 2% net-10 discounts that were previously missed.

Azure OCR accuracy on invoice PDFs was 96% for structured invoices but dropped to 81% for handwritten or poorly formatted supplier invoices — the 19% of suppliers with messy invoices generated 44% of OCR-related exceptions.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that AI should auto-resolve exceptions. Discovery showed that 41% of exceptions were caused by upstream data entry errors in the PO system, not genuine invoice discrepancies — the real fix was catching errors at PO creation, not resolving them at invoice matching. Still automated the downstream resolution, but added PO validation rules that prevented 35% of exceptions from occurring.
implementation_friction: SAP integration required a custom RFC function module because the standard BAPI didn't expose exception reason codes — SAP developer availability was limited, causing a 2.5 week delay.

Supplier master data had 1,400 duplicate entries across 3 company codes — data cleansing was a prerequisite that took 3 weeks and required accounts payable, procurement, and master data teams to collaborate.

Finance leadership initially wanted zero-touch auto-approval for resolved exceptions, but audit requirements mandated a review step for invoices over $10K — redesigning the approval workflow added 1 week.

title: Predictive Fleet Maintenance Scheduling
problem: Fleet of 120 trucks experienced 34 unplanned breakdowns per quarter costing $18K each in downtime and expedited repair. Maintenance was purely calendar-based.
conditions_for_success: Telematics sensors installed on all vehicles
12+ months of maintenance history in structured format
Maintenance manager willing to shift from calendar to condition-based scheduling
anti_patterns: Do not deploy if sensor data coverage is under 70% of fleet
Avoid if maintenance history is in paper logs without digitization plan
generalized_for: Any asset-heavy business using calendar-based maintenance on equipment with sensor telemetry — applies to airlines, mining operations, HVAC companies, and elevator service providers.
lessons_learned: The predictive model caught 58% of breakdowns, but 23% of its 'imminent failure' alerts were false alarms in the first month — maintenance crews wasted 4 hours per false alarm pulling trucks off routes for inspection. Tuning the alert threshold from 0.6 to 0.75 confidence reduced false alarms to 9% while only dropping catch rate to 51%.

Brake system failures were nearly impossible to predict from telematics alone (only 34% accuracy) because they depended on route terrain and load weight, which weren't in the sensor data — had to exclude brake failures from the predictive model and keep them on calendar-based maintenance.

The biggest maintenance cost savings came not from preventing breakdowns but from extending the intervals on healthy trucks — the model showed that 40% of calendar-based oil changes were unnecessary, saving $280 per truck per year in parts and labor.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that predicting breakdowns would save $18K per avoided breakdown. Discovery showed that the calendar-based maintenance schedule was both too frequent for healthy trucks and too infrequent for heavily-used ones — the real opportunity was right-sizing maintenance intervals per vehicle, which saved more annually than breakdown prevention alone.
implementation_friction: 22 of 120 trucks had older telematics units that reported data every 15 minutes instead of every 60 seconds — the sparse data made predictions unreliable for those vehicles, requiring hardware upgrades that took 4 weeks and cost $18K.
Maintenance history was split between Samsara (recent) and a legacy Access database (pre-2021) — merging and deduplicating 3 years of records took 2.5 weeks.

Maintenance manager initially resisted condition-based scheduling because it disrupted the shop's workflow — required a 2-week parallel-run where both calendars were maintained simultaneously before he trusted the new approach.

title: Inventory Demand Forecasting for Retail Chain
problem: Merchandising team used 12-week trailing average for reorder decisions. Stockout rate was 8.4% on top-50 SKUs; overstock tied up $2.1M in working capital annually.
conditions_for_success: 2+ years of POS transaction history by SKU and location
Promotional calendar integrated into demand model
Merchandising team trained to interpret forecast confidence intervals
anti_patterns: Do not use single-model forecast for highly seasonal specialty SKUs without manual override
Avoid deploying if supplier lead times are unreliable — fix supply chain first
generalized_for: Any business that holds physical inventory and makes reorder decisions based on demand estimates — applies to restaurant chains, auto parts distributors, pharmaceutical wholesalers, and e-commerce fulfillment centers.
lessons_learned: Forecast accuracy improved 31% overall, but for new products (launched in last 90 days) the model was worse than the merchandiser's intuition — new products had no sales history, so the model defaulted to category averages that were often wrong by 40%+. Required a manual override pathway for new product launches.

The $890K working capital release came primarily from 12 SKUs that were chronically overstocked — these 12 items (out of 3,200 total) represented 42% of the excess inventory. A simple reorder point adjustment for just these SKUs would have captured half the benefit without any AI.

Promotional demand spikes were the hardest to forecast — the model underestimated promo uplift by 22% on average because historical promo data didn't capture the specific marketing channel mix used. Adding promo channel tags as features improved uplift prediction by 15 points.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that better forecasting would reduce stockouts. Discovery showed stockouts on top-50 SKUs cost $1.4M/year in lost sales, but the $2.1M in tied-up working capital from overstock was the bigger problem — and it was concentrated in slow-moving long-tail SKUs, not the top 50. The real ROI was reducing overstock on the long tail, not preventing stockouts on the head.
implementation_friction: NetSuite's reporting API had a 10,000-row export limit per call — extracting 2 years of transaction data across 3,200 SKUs and 14 locations required building a paginated extraction pipeline that took 1.5 weeks.

Promotional calendar was maintained in a marketing team's Google Sheet with inconsistent date formats and no SKU linkage — normalizing and linking promotions to SKUs took 2 weeks of back-and-forth with the marketing team.

Merchandising team initially ignored forecast confidence intervals and treated point estimates as exact predictions — required 3 training sessions over 2 weeks before the team started using intervals to make risk-adjusted reorder decisions.

title: Customer Service Chatbot for Retail Returns
problem: Customer service team handled 1,400 contacts per week; 62% were routine returns and order status inquiries. Agents spent 3 minutes per routine contact, leaving complex issues under-served.
conditions_for_success: Returns policy documented and consistent across channels
Order management system has API for real-time status
Clear escalation path for unhappy customers to human agent
anti_patterns: Avoid if returns policy has many exceptions requiring judgment
Do not deploy without fallback to human agent — dead-ends destroy satisfaction
generalized_for: Any business with high-volume repetitive customer inquiries where most questions have deterministic answers — applies to telecom providers, utility companies, subscription box services, and travel booking platforms.
lessons_learned: 68% deflection rate was achieved for returns and order status, but the chatbot only deflected 12% of product questions because customers asked highly specific questions ('will this fit a 2019 Honda Civic trunk?') that required product knowledge the bot didn't have — scoping the bot to returns+status only was key to high satisfaction.

Customer satisfaction improved 0.4 points overall, but customers who were escalated from the bot to a human agent had 0.2 points lower satisfaction than those who reached a human directly — the bot-to-human handoff felt like 'starting over' until a context-passing feature was added in week 5.

24% of routine contacts were actually 'routine question + emotional frustration' — the bot resolved the question correctly but the customer still wanted to vent, leading to repeated escalation requests. Adding an empathy acknowledgment step before resolution reduced unnecessary escalations by 18%.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that deflecting routine contacts would save agent time. Discovery showed the real problem was that complex issues (38% of contacts) had a 42-minute average handling time because agents were constantly interrupted by routine questions — freeing agents from routine work reduced complex-issue resolution time by 28%, which had a bigger CSAT impact than the chatbot itself.
implementation_friction: Shopify's order API had a 2-second response time for historical orders (>90 days), causing chatbot timeout issues — required implementing an order cache that added 1 week of development.

Returns policy documentation was spread across 4 Notion pages, a PDF, and tribal knowledge held by 2 senior agents — consolidating into a structured knowledge base took 1.5 weeks and revealed 6 policy contradictions.

Intercom's bot builder had a 20-step conversation limit that was too restrictive for complex return flows — had to use the API-based custom bot framework instead of the visual builder, adding 1 week of development.

title: Quality Inspection Defect Detection
problem: Visual inspection line had 2.3% defect escape rate on PCB assemblies; each escaped defect cost $2,400 in field rework or warranty claim. 6 inspectors did 100% manual checks.
conditions_for_success: Minimum 2,000 labelled defect images per defect class
Consistent lighting and camera setup on production line
MES integration for traceability from inspection to shipment
anti_patterns: Do not use for low-volume, highly variable custom assemblies without retraining plan
Avoid if lighting or product orientation varies significantly without standardization
generalized_for: Any production environment where visual inspection is a bottleneck and defect escapes have high downstream costs — applies to food packaging lines, textile manufacturing, pharmaceutical tablet inspection, and automotive paint shops.
lessons_learned: The vision model achieved 97% detection accuracy on solder bridge defects but only 82% on hairline cracks — hairline cracks required 5x more training images (10,000+) because they varied enormously with PCB color, component density, and crack orientation. Had to keep human inspectors for crack detection in parallel for 8 weeks while collecting more training data.

Inspection throughput improved 40% but created a downstream bottleneck at the rework station — defects were now caught faster than they could be repaired, creating a queue that grew to 200 boards in week 2. Required adding a second rework technician.

Camera placement precision was critical: a 2mm shift in camera position caused a 12% accuracy drop on one line because shadows from nearby components changed — required building a rigid mounting jig and adding a daily calibration check, which added 1 week to deployment.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that AI vision would catch more defects than human inspectors. Discovery showed that human inspectors actually caught 97.7% of defects — their escape rate was only 2.3%. But they achieved this by spending 45 seconds per board at 100% inspection. The real opportunity was maintaining the same catch rate at 3x throughput, not improving the catch rate itself.
implementation_friction: Collecting 2,000 labelled defect images per class took 6 weeks because the defect rate was only 2.3% — had to run the line at full speed while manually capturing and labelling defects, requiring 2 inspectors to do double-duty.
MES integration required a custom API adapter because the MES vendor (Aegis) charged $25K for their standard integration module — built a lightweight REST adapter instead, but it took 2 weeks.

Production line lighting varied by 15% intensity over the course of a day due to skylight exposure — required installing blackout panels and dedicated LED arrays, which took 1.5 weeks and $4K in materials.

title: Predictive Maintenance for CNC Equipment
problem: Plant had 18 CNC machines averaging 2.8 unplanned stops per machine per month; each stop cost $8,500 in lost production. Maintenance was time-based with no condition monitoring.
conditions_for_success: Vibration and temperature sensors installed on all critical machines
Historian data available for 18+ months
Maintenance planner involved from day one
anti_patterns: Do not deploy on machines with under 6 months sensor history
Avoid if plant IT cannot support edge computing nodes for real-time inference
generalized_for: Any facility operating high-value machinery where unplanned downtime has a measurable per-hour cost — applies to data centers, commercial printing plants, semiconductor fabs, and oil refineries.
lessons_learned: Spindle bearing failures were the most predictable failure mode (81% accuracy at 72-hour lead time), but coolant system failures were nearly random (38% accuracy) because they were caused by external contamination events the sensors couldn't detect — had to exclude coolant failures from the predictive model and keep them on time-based inspection.

The 63% reduction in unplanned stops was achieved only after 4 months of model operation — the first 2 months showed only 41% because the model needed to observe at least one full failure cycle per machine to calibrate its thresholds. Early results disappointed the plant manager and nearly killed the project.

OEE improvement of 6.4% was worth $1.07M annually, but 2.1% of that came from an unexpected source: reduced setup time. When maintenance was predictable, the shop floor could schedule maintenance during planned changeovers instead of interrupting production runs.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that predicting failures would prevent downtime. Discovery showed that 40% of unplanned stops were actually caused by operators running machines outside recommended parameters (too high feed rate, insufficient coolant flow) — the predictive model caught these operator-induced stress patterns 12-24 hours before failure, enabling process corrections rather than just maintenance alerts.
implementation_friction: Siemens MindSphere data export API had a 4-hour latency for historical data, making model training iterations painfully slow — each training run required a full day of data fetching. Built a local InfluxDB mirror that added 2 weeks but accelerated all subsequent work.
4 of 18 CNC machines were older models (pre-2015) with proprietary sensor protocols that MindSphere couldn't ingest natively — required custom protocol adapters at $3K per machine, adding 2.5 weeks.

Plant IT refused to allow edge computing nodes on the shop floor network due to cybersecurity concerns — required a 3-week security review and the addition of a network DMZ that isolated the inference nodes from the production control network.

title: Supplier Risk Monitoring and Alerting
problem: Procurement team tracked 240 Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers manually using quarterly scorecards. Two supply disruptions per year cost $1.8M each in expedite fees and line stops.
conditions_for_success: Supplier master data in ERP is complete and current
Procurement team will act on alerts within 48 hours
External data feeds (news, financial, logistics) accessible
anti_patterns: Do not treat alerts as automated decisions — always requires procurement judgment
Avoid if supplier relationships are informal and not tracked in ERP
generalized_for: Any organization with a critical supplier network where disruptions cascade into operational losses — applies to restaurant chains managing food suppliers, hospitals managing medical device vendors, and construction firms managing subcontractors.
lessons_learned: News-based signals detected 71% of disruptions (port closures, factory fires, bankruptcy filings), but the remaining 29% were caused by quality degradation that didn't make the news — had to add a 'delivery performance trend' signal from SAP receiving data that caught gradual supplier decline over 6-8 weeks.

The system generated 14 alerts per week initially, of which only 3 were actionable — alert fatigue set in by week 3 and the procurement team started ignoring alerts. Reduced alerts to 5/week by raising the severity threshold and adding a 'recommended action' to each alert, which restored engagement.

83% of disruption events had mitigation plans, but only 62% of those plans were executed in time because the procurement team's approval workflow required VP sign-off for alternate sourcing — streamlining the approval chain for pre-approved alternates was as important as the alerting system itself.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that earlier detection of supplier risks would prevent disruptions. Discovery showed the company already knew about most Tier 1 risks through direct relationships — the blind spot was Tier 2 suppliers (suppliers' suppliers) where they had zero visibility. 60% of the $1.8M disruption costs came from Tier 2 failures. Shifted focus from 'monitor our suppliers' to 'monitor our suppliers' suppliers'.
implementation_friction: SAP Ariba's supplier data had only 156 of 240 suppliers with complete records — 84 suppliers were missing financial identifiers needed for external risk feed matching, requiring a 3-week data enrichment effort with the procurement team.

News API (GNews) had a 100-request/day limit on the free tier, insufficient for monitoring 240 suppliers — upgrading to the paid tier required procurement approval that took 2 weeks due to vendor onboarding processes.

Procurement team's quarterly scorecard process was deeply embedded in their workflow — convincing them to shift to continuous monitoring required 2 weeks of parallel-running both systems and demonstrating that the real-time system caught a $400K risk the scorecard would have missed.

title: Proposal Generation Copilot for Consulting Firm
problem: Senior consultants spent 12 hours per proposal on boilerplate sections. Win rate was 34%; post-mortem showed proposals lacked specificity to client context. 3 proposals per week average.
conditions_for_success: Past proposal library accessible in structured format
CRM data with won/lost outcome labels available
Senior consultants willing to review and edit AI drafts
anti_patterns: Do not auto-send proposals without human review
Avoid if past proposal library is under 100 documents
generalized_for: Any services business that competes through written proposals or bids — applies to architecture firms, advertising agencies, IT staffing companies, and government contractors.
lessons_learned: The copilot produced excellent boilerplate but struggled with the 'why us' section that required firm-specific differentiation — 78% of consultant edits were in this section, which was only 15% of the proposal by word count. Building a 'firm capabilities' knowledge base specifically for this section reduced edits to 41%.

Win rate improvement of 11% was entirely driven by proposals that were previously 'won on merit but lost on timing' — faster drafting meant the firm submitted 2.3 days earlier on average, and in competitive bids, early submission correlated with 18% higher win rates.

Consultants initially spent 3.5 hours per proposal editing AI drafts, nearly as long as writing from scratch for short proposals — the time savings only became significant for proposals over 20 pages. For sub-10-page proposals, the copilot was actually slower due to editing overhead.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that faster proposal drafting would free consultant time. Discovery showed the real problem was that consultants were recycling old proposals with find-and-replace, creating generic-sounding bids. The copilot's actual value was pulling relevant case studies and metrics from past wins to create client-specific proposals — the quality improvement drove the win rate increase more than the time savings.
implementation_friction: Past proposal library had 340 documents across SharePoint, Google Drive, and local folders — consolidating them into a searchable repository took 2 weeks and revealed that 28% were duplicates or obsolete versions.

Salesforce opportunity records lacked 'loss reason' data for 65% of lost deals — without this data, the model couldn't learn what made proposals fail. Required a 1-week effort to retroactively tag loss reasons from post-mortem notes.

Two senior partners insisted on their own proposal templates that differed from the firm standard — accommodating partner-specific formatting preferences required building template variants, adding 1 week.

title: Automated Time Tracking and Project Allocation
problem: Consultants manually entered time across 8 project codes after the fact; 22% of time was logged late or to the wrong code. Finance close took 4 extra days each month for corrections.
conditions_for_success: Calendar and email metadata accessible for inference (with consent)
Project code taxonomy is stable and well-defined
Finance team agrees to review-and-confirm workflow rather than fully automated posting
anti_patterns: Avoid full automation of time posting — consultant confirmation required for accuracy
Do not deploy if project codes change frequently without governance process
generalized_for: Any organization where professionals bill time to projects or clients and manual time entry creates revenue leakage — applies to law firms, accounting practices, marketing agencies, and IT managed service providers.
lessons_learned: Calendar-based time inference was 89% accurate for meetings (clear attendee lists mapped to projects), but only 61% accurate for 'deep work' blocks where consultants worked on multiple projects without calendar entries — had to add a daily 2-minute confirmation step for non-meeting time, which consultants accepted because it replaced a 15-minute daily time entry task.

The $180K in write-off reduction came from catching time that was never logged at all, not from correcting misallocations — 14% of billable hours were simply lost because consultants forgot to log them. The system detected these gaps by comparing calendar activity to time entries.

Finance close improvement of 3.2 days was partially offset by a new review bottleneck — finance now had to approve auto-suggested allocations instead of correcting manual entries. Net improvement was 3.2 days but required retraining the finance team on the new review workflow, which took 1 week.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that misallocated time caused billing inaccuracies. Discovery showed the bigger problem was unbilled time — consultants consistently under-reported hours by 11% on average because they rounded down or forgot short tasks. Capturing this 'missing' 11% at a blended billing rate of $185/hour was worth more than fixing the misallocation problem.
implementation_friction: Microsoft Teams API required Azure AD admin consent for calendar and email metadata access — the consent request triggered a privacy review that took 3 weeks, during which no development could proceed on the inference engine.

Dynamics 365 project module had a non-standard time entry schema that didn't match the API documentation — required 1.5 weeks of reverse-engineering the actual data model with the ERP vendor's support team.

Consultants in the London office were subject to GDPR constraints on email metadata processing that US consultants were not — required building a region-specific data handling pipeline that added 1 week and reduced inference accuracy for UK staff by 8%.

title: Resource Allocation Optimization for Project Staffing
problem: Staffing manager allocated 160 consultants to projects using a spreadsheet. Utilization averaged 67%; 28 skill mismatches per quarter led to project delays and client escalations.
conditions_for_success: Skills database current and consultant-verified
Project staffing needs defined at least 2 weeks ahead
Staffing manager uses tool as advisory, retains final decision
anti_patterns: Do not automate staffing decisions without manager override capability
Avoid if project requirements are too vague for structured skill matching
generalized_for: Any organization that matches skilled workers to project-based assignments — applies to staffing agencies, engineering firms, film production companies, and hospital shift scheduling.
lessons_learned: The skills database listed 340 unique skills but only 45 were actually used in staffing decisions — the remaining 295 were too granular (e.g., 'Python 3.9' vs 'Python 3.10') and created false mismatches. Collapsing to 60 skill clusters improved matching accuracy from 71% to 89%.

Utilization improvement of 9% was achieved primarily by filling 'gap weeks' — the model identified that 23% of consultants had 1-2 week gaps between project assignments that the staffing manager couldn't see in the spreadsheet. These gaps represented $940K in lost revenue annually.

28 skill mismatches per quarter were concentrated on 4 project types: data engineering, cloud migration, security audit, and UX research — these domains had the most specialized skill requirements. Generic matching worked fine for the other 12 project types.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that better skill matching would reduce project failures. Discovery showed that the staffing manager actually knew the skill requirements well — the problem was that she couldn't see availability across 160 consultants in a spreadsheet. 72% of mismatches occurred because the best-fit consultant was available but the manager didn't know it. The tool's value was visibility, not intelligence.
implementation_friction: Salesforce PSA had consultant availability data, but it was 2 weeks stale because project managers didn't update end dates when projects ran over — required building a reconciliation process that cross-referenced timesheets with PSA records, adding 1.5 weeks.
The internal skills database hadn't been updated by 40% of consultants in over a year — running a skills self-assessment campaign took 3 weeks and required HR involvement to achieve 92% completion.
Staffing manager was the sole user and gatekeeper — her 2-week vacation in the middle of the project froze all user testing and feedback, effectively extending the timeline by 2 weeks.

title: Retail Shrinkage Detection and Alert System
problem: Loss prevention team reviewed 2TB of CCTV footage weekly to identify shrinkage events. Monthly shrinkage ran 1.8% of revenue; detection lagged 2-3 weeks behind incidents.
conditions_for_success: IP camera network with consistent resolution (1080p minimum)
Loss prevention team willing to act on real-time alerts
Privacy legal review completed before deployment
anti_patterns: Do not deploy without legal sign-off on biometric/identity implications
Avoid if camera coverage has significant blind spots in high-shrinkage zones
generalized_for: Any business using video surveillance for security or compliance where manual footage review is impractical — applies to warehouse operations, parking facilities, construction sites, and casino gaming floors.
lessons_learned: The vision model detected 84% of external theft events but only 52% of internal (employee) shrinkage because employee theft patterns were subtler — small quantity removals, void transactions, and sweet-hearting required POS data correlation, not just video analysis. Adding POS anomaly detection alongside video pushed internal theft detection to 71%.

False alert rate was 23% in the first month, generating 45 false alerts per week — LP analysts started ignoring alerts by week 2. Tuning the model specifically for each store's layout and camera angles reduced false alerts to 7% by month 2, but this per-store tuning took 3 days per location across 8 stores.

31% shrinkage reduction was partly deterrent effect — when employees learned the system existed, internal shrinkage dropped 18% in the first 2 weeks before the model was even accurate, suggesting that visible monitoring was as valuable as actual detection.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that faster detection of theft events would reduce losses. Discovery showed that 62% of shrinkage was not theft at all — it was process errors: incorrect receiving counts, damaged goods not written off, and vendor short-shipments not caught at delivery. Redirecting LP analyst time from video review to process audits (enabled by AI handling video) addressed the larger shrinkage category.
implementation_friction: Privacy legal review took 5 weeks because the system's person-detection capability triggered biometric data regulations in 2 of the 4 operating states — required building a 'no facial recognition' mode that detected behavioral patterns without identifying individuals.

Milestone VMS exported video in a proprietary codec that required transcoding before the vision model could process it — building the transcoding pipeline added 2 weeks and required a dedicated GPU server for real-time processing.

Camera angles at 3 of 8 stores had significant blind spots at self-checkout areas, which were the highest-shrinkage zones — adding 12 cameras across 3 stores cost $18K and took 2 weeks for installation and network configuration.

title: SaaS Customer Onboarding Automation
problem: SaaS platform with 12,000 new signups per month had a 68% drop-off before activation. Onboarding team of 15 manually emailed and called new users. Activation-to-paid conversion was 23%.
conditions_for_success: Product usage analytics pipeline in place (Segment/Amplitude or equivalent) with event-level granularity
CRM integration with lifecycle stage tracking
Customer segmentation model that distinguishes self-serve vs enterprise signups
Billing system API access for conversion event tracking
anti_patterns: Do not automate enterprise onboarding — accounts with >50 seats need human relationship management; automating these dropped enterprise conversion by 22% in a failed A/B test
Avoid if product has no usage tracking — without knowing what users do after signup, the automation sends generic messages that perform worse than no messages at all
Do not send more than 5 automated touchpoints in the first 14 days — engagement data shows open rates collapse by 70% after the 5th message
generalized_for: Any product-led growth company with high signup volume but low activation, where the gap between signup and value realization can be bridged with targeted nudges.
lessons_learned: The initial automation sent the same onboarding sequence to all users, which improved activation by only 8% — segmenting users by their first-session behavior (explored API docs vs used dashboard vs imported data) and sending role-specific sequences pushed activation improvement to 34%. The segmentation logic took 3 weeks to build and test but delivered 4x the impact.

15 onboarding team members were doing 3 distinct jobs: welcome calls, troubleshooting setup issues, and chasing inactive signups. Automation replaced the chasing (40% of their time) and welcome calls (35%), but troubleshooting setup issues (25%) required human judgment and actually increased in volume because more users were now reaching the setup stage.

Activation-to-paid conversion improved 41%, but 60% of that improvement came from reducing time-to-activation from 8.3 days to 4.1 days — users who activated within 3 days converted at 38% vs 14% for users who took 10+ days. Speed-to-value was more important than any specific onboarding content.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that better onboarding emails would reduce drop-off. Discovery showed that 43% of drop-offs happened at a single friction point: the API key generation step required users to create a separate developer account. Users who made it past this step activated at 81%. The biggest ROI came from redesigning this one step (a product change, not an AI change) while the automation handled everything around it.
implementation_friction: Segment event taxonomy had 340 events but no consistent naming convention — 'user_signup', 'signup_complete', and 'registration_done' all meant the same thing across different product surfaces. Harmonizing the event taxonomy took 2.5 weeks and required coordination with 3 product teams.

Amplitude cohort export API had a 24-hour delay, making real-time behavior-triggered messaging impossible — required building a custom event listener on the Segment stream for time-sensitive triggers, adding 2 weeks.

Intercom's automation builder couldn't handle conditional branching based on product usage events — required using the Intercom API to build custom workflows, which tripled the implementation time for the messaging layer from 1 week to 3 weeks.

title: Developer Documentation Copilot
problem: Engineering team maintained 4,000+ API documentation pages. 30% were outdated within 3 months of code changes. Developers spent 6 hours per week answering questions already covered in docs.
conditions_for_success: Documentation stored in version control (git-based like Docusaurus, GitBook, or Readme.io)
Code-to-documentation linking possible via file paths, function names, or API route mapping
Engineering leadership buy-in for doc review as part of PR workflow
Search infrastructure in place (Algolia or ElasticSearch)
anti_patterns: Do not auto-publish AI-generated doc updates without engineering review — a single incorrect API parameter description caused 3 days of debugging for 12 external developers in a pilot test
Avoid if documentation is in unstructured wikis (Confluence, Notion) without page-to-code mapping — the copilot needs to know which code change affects which doc page
Do not use for auto-generating architecture decision docs — these require context the copilot cannot infer from code alone
generalized_for: Any organization with a large technical knowledge base that drifts out of sync with the systems it describes — applies to hardware manufacturers with service manuals, regulated industries with SOPs, and platform companies with partner integration guides.
lessons_learned: The copilot successfully detected 72% of stale docs by diffing code changes against doc content, but 18% of its 'stale' flags were false positives caused by refactored code that didn't change behavior — internal variable renames triggered unnecessary doc review cycles that cost 4 hours/week until a 'behavior-change-only' filter was added.

Developer question reduction of 58% was concentrated in Slack channels — the copilot answered questions in under 30 seconds that previously waited 2-4 hours for a human response. But 23% of copilot answers were subtly wrong (correct syntax, wrong context), which eroded trust until a confidence-scoring and 'verified answer' badge system was added in week 6.

New engineer onboarding time dropped 2.3 weeks, but the mechanism was unexpected — new engineers used the copilot as a 'explain this codebase' tool rather than reading docs directly. They asked questions like 'how does auth work in this service?' and got contextual answers, effectively creating personalized onboarding paths.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that keeping docs fresh would reduce developer confusion. Discovery showed that developers rarely read docs proactively — they searched for answers when stuck. 74% of doc page views came from search, not browsing. The real opportunity was building a question-answering interface over the docs, not just keeping the docs fresh. Freshness was a prerequisite, not the solution.
implementation_friction: Mapping 4,000 doc pages to source code files required building a custom linker that traced API routes in documentation to endpoint handlers in code — the initial regex-based approach had 34% accuracy and required a code-AST-based approach that took 3 weeks to build correctly.

Docusaurus's build pipeline ran in 8 minutes, but adding the staleness-check step increased it to 22 minutes — engineers complained about slow doc builds. Required moving the check to an async post-build job that reported results via Slack, adding 1 week.

Algolia's free tier had a 10,000-record limit that was insufficient for 4,000 pages with section-level indexing — upgrading to the paid plan required procurement approval and a 2-week vendor onboarding process.

title: Platform Fraud and Abuse Detection
problem: Trust and safety team reviewed 8,000 flagged accounts per week manually. False positive rate was 72%. Account takeover detection lagged 4 hours. Platform processed $50B+ in transactions annually.
conditions_for_success: Historical fraud labels with at least 18 months of data and clear taxonomy (fraud type, severity, resolution)
Real-time event pipeline (Kafka or equivalent) capable of sub-second event delivery
Compliance and legal sign-off on AI-assisted account actions — especially for regulated payment flows
Existing rule engine that can be augmented (not replaced) as a fallback
anti_patterns: Do not fully automate account suspension — a wrongful suspension on a $2M/month merchant caused a legal claim and 8% churn in that merchant segment during a competitor's pilot. Always require human confirmation for account-level actions above a risk threshold
Avoid deploying if regulatory environment requires fully explainable decisions and the model uses deep embeddings — use a gradient-boosted model with feature importance instead
Do not remove legacy rules entirely — 14% of fraud patterns are too rare for ML to learn but are well-captured by expert-written rules. Hybrid systems outperform pure ML by 8% on these edge cases
generalized_for: Any platform that must distinguish legitimate from malicious activity at high volume and speed — applies to social media platforms detecting fake accounts, e-commerce marketplaces detecting seller fraud, and ad networks detecting click fraud.
lessons_learned: The 72% false positive rate was caused by 5 legacy rules written in 2019 that flagged behavior patterns that were once rare but became normal — e.g., 'multiple login locations in 24 hours' triggered constantly for mobile users switching between Wi-Fi and cellular. Disabling just these 5 rules (before any ML) dropped the false positive rate to 51%.

Account takeover detection latency dropped from 4 hours to 22 minutes, but the first 3 weeks of deployment saw 340 false ATO alerts because the model wasn't trained on legitimate device changes during the holiday season — users getting new phones triggered ATO alerts. Required a seasonal retraining cycle and a 'new device grace period' that added 1.5 weeks of tuning.

Analyst productivity improved 73% because the copilot pre-investigated each flagged account — pulling transaction history, device fingerprints, and behavioral comparisons into a single view. Analysts previously spent 65% of their review time gathering this context across 4 different tools.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that better fraud detection models would reduce losses. Discovery showed that $50M+ in annual fraud losses broke down as: 40% from account takeovers, 35% from synthetic identity fraud, and 25% from policy abuse (returns fraud, promo stacking). But the trust and safety team spent 70% of their time on the 25% policy abuse category because it had the highest flag volume. Refocusing the team on ATO (higher dollar impact per case) while automating policy abuse triage delivered 3x more dollar savings.
implementation_friction: Kafka event pipeline delivered events with variable latency (p50=200ms, p99=4.2s) — the p99 tail caused the model to miss time-sensitive ATO patterns in 6% of cases. Required adding a Redis-based event buffer and reprocessing window, adding 2.5 weeks.

Historical fraud labels had a 9% label error rate — confirmed by manual audit of 500 cases. Accounts marked 'fraud' that were actually policy violations, and accounts marked 'clean' that had undetected fraud. Required a 2-week label cleaning effort with the trust and safety team before model training could begin.

Legal team required a 4-week review of the model's decision factors to ensure compliance with payment card industry (PCI) regulations and state money transmission laws — this was the single longest delay in the project and could not be parallelized with engineering work.

title: SaaS Revenue Forecasting and Churn Prediction
problem: Rev ops team used spreadsheet models for quarterly revenue forecasting. Forecast accuracy was plus or minus 18%. Churn detection was reactive, identifying churning customers 2 weeks after warning signals appeared.
conditions_for_success: Minimum 2 years of billing and subscription data with clear churn event definitions
Product usage telemetry at the account level (daily active users, feature adoption, login frequency)
CRM pipeline data with historical stage progression and close dates
Customer success team willing to act on early churn signals within 48 hours
anti_patterns: Do not use for companies with fewer than 100 customers — the model needs statistical significance and small customer bases produce unreliable predictions. At 80 customers, the model's false positive rate was 45% vs 12% at 500+

Avoid if pricing model changes more than twice per year — frequent pricing changes invalidate historical revenue patterns and require continuous model retraining that costs more than the forecast improvement is worth

Do not auto-trigger churn intervention sequences without CS team review — a premature 'we noticed you're using us less' email to a customer who was actually just on vacation caused a complaint escalation to the CEO
generalized_for: Any subscription or recurring-revenue business that needs to forecast revenue and identify at-risk customers before renewal — applies to insurance carriers, gym chains, media subscription services, and managed IT providers.
lessons_learned: Forecast accuracy improved from plus-or-minus 18% to plus-or-minus 6.8%, but the model's biggest contribution wasn't the point estimate — it was identifying which deals in the pipeline were likely to slip. 34% of forecast error came from 'committed' deals that slipped to the next quarter. The model flagged 71% of these slip-risk deals 3 weeks before quarter-end, giving sales leadership time to intervene.

Churn prediction model identified product usage decline as the strongest signal (3.2x more predictive than support ticket volume), but the definition of 'usage decline' was different for each customer segment — enterprise customers with seasonal businesses naturally had low-usage quarters that weren't churn signals. Required segment-specific baselines that took 2 weeks to calibrate.

19% churn rate reduction was achieved by catching customers 8 weeks before renewal instead of 2 weeks — the CS team's intervention success rate was 42% at 8 weeks lead time vs 11% at 2 weeks. The model didn't make the CS team better at saving customers; it gave them enough time to actually have the conversation.
discovery_insight: Initial hypothesis was that better forecasting models would improve forecast accuracy. Discovery showed that 60% of forecast error came from CRM pipeline data quality, not model limitations — sales reps had outdated close dates (38% were overdue), inflated deal amounts (23% adjusted down at close), and stale stage progressions (41% hadn't been updated in 30+ days). Cleaning pipeline hygiene through automated CRM nudges delivered half the accuracy improvement before the forecasting model was even deployed.
implementation_friction: Snowflake had billing data and Salesforce had pipeline data, but joining them required matching company names across systems — 22% of accounts had different names in each system (abbreviations, legal entity names vs trade names). Building a fuzzy matching pipeline took 2.5 weeks and still required manual review of 140 ambiguous matches.

Amplitude's account-level usage aggregation wasn't configured — usage data was at the user level, and mapping users to accounts required the Amplitude-to-Salesforce identity resolution integration, which had a 3-week setup and testing period.

The rev ops team's existing spreadsheet model was treated as 'ground truth' by the CFO — gaining confidence to replace it required running both models in parallel for 2 quarters (6 months of shadow mode), during which the new model proved more accurate in 5 of 6 months. This shadow period wasn't in the original timeline but was essential for organizational adoption.
